stem: A 42-year-old man presents to a Hunter New England LHD emergency department after his wife found a note on the kitchen bench. He has a 6-month history of low mood following job loss, has been drinking more in the evenings, and tonight researched paracetamol overdose dosing on his phone before his wife intervened. He denies a specific plan now but says he 'sees no other way'. He has a stored firearm at home (legally licensed for sport shooting) and is currently sober. He has no past psychiatric admission, no prior self-harm, and his GP started sertraline 50 mg daily 2 weeks ago. His brother died by suicide aged 38. The ED registrar asks you to structure your initial risk assessment.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, slow speech, eye contact intermittent, no thought disorder, no perceptual abnormality elicited.
data_table.Investigations: BAC 0.00, paracetamol level not yet sent; basic obs within normal limits.

lead_in: Which approach most accurately frames a structured suicide risk assessment in this presentation?

options.text: Use the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale to characterise ideation, intent, plan and behaviour
options.rationale: The Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) is the structured framework most widely endorsed in Australian and international guidance: it separates passive ideation, active ideation without plan, ideation with method, ideation with intent, and ideation with plan-and-intent, then enumerates preparatory acts and interrupted, aborted and actual attempts. It captures both severity and behaviour rather than yielding a single risk score and is the basis for triage decisions across NSW Health mental health services.
source_refs: NSW Health Suicide Risk Assessment and Management Framework

options.text: Categorise him as low, medium or high risk based on overall clinical impression
options.rationale: RANZCP and recent NSW Health guidance explicitly move away from low/medium/high categorical risk stratification because it has poor predictive validity and creates false reassurance. Would be a deprecated approach (Source: RANZCP CPG 2016).

options.text: Calculate his SAD PERSONS score to determine inpatient admission
options.rationale: SAD PERSONS lacks predictive validity for individual decisions and is not recommended as a sole decision aid by current Australian guidance. Would be wrong as the structured framework (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Defer formal assessment until the on-call psychiatry registrar is available
options.rationale: The ED clinician must conduct an initial risk assessment to inform immediate safety; psychiatry input supplements rather than replaces this. Would be unsafe (Source: NSW Health Framework).
source_refs: NSW Health Suicide Risk Assessment and Management Framework

options.text: Ask only whether he plans to act in the next 24 hours and document the response
options.rationale: A single-question approach misses ideation, intent, plan, access to means and behaviour; structured assessment is required. Would be inadequate (Source: RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: Australian suicide-risk practice has moved from categorical low/medium/high stratification to structured assessment of ideation severity, intent, plan, behaviour, dynamic and static risk factors, and access to means, with safety planning tailored to modifiable drivers. The C-SSRS is the most widely used structured framework, with the Chronological Assessment of Suicide Events (CASE) approach as the complementary interview technique.
key_points: Categorical low/medium/high stratification is deprecated
C-SSRS separates ideation by intent and plan and counts behaviours
Static factors set baseline risk; dynamic factors are what intervention targets
Access to means (firearms, medications) must be addressed
Family history of suicide is a non-trivial static risk factor
explanation.pearls: Asking about suicide does not plant the idea; explicit, calm questioning increases disclosure.

sources.label: NSW Health Suicide Risk Assessment and Management Framework

subtopic_detail: Structured assessment framework vs categorical risk stratification

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to her GP at a Central Coast LHD community health centre with worsening depression. She has a 10-year history of major depressive disorder, previous overdose at age 23, current relationship breakdown 4 weeks ago, recent loss of employment, and increased alcohol consumption (now 6-8 standard drinks most nights). She lives alone, has access to a stockpile of zopiclone and amitriptyline left over from a past prescription, and tells the GP she has thought about driving her car into a tree. She has supportive parents in the same suburb who do not know how she has been feeling. The GP asks which factors are most amenable to intervention before her next appointment in 3 days.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, mildly disengaged, no psychomotor retardation.

lead_in: Which combination best represents the modifiable (dynamic) risk factors the GP should target now?

options.text: Access to the zopiclone and amitriptyline stockpile, current alcohol intake, social isolation
options.rationale: Means restriction (removing the stockpiled medications), substance use reduction, and reducing social isolation through enlisting her parents are the highest-yield dynamic interventions in the 72-hour window. All three are modifiable, evidence-supported, and actionable in primary care; tricyclic stockpile is a recognised means of lethal overdose.
source_refs: Mann et al 2005 means restriction review

options.text: Previous overdose at age 23, family psychiatric history, female sex
options.rationale: These are static (non-modifiable) risk factors; they inform baseline risk but cannot be changed by intervention. Would not address what is actionable now (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: 10-year MDD history, recent job loss, age
options.rationale: Job loss is partially modifiable but at the structural level; MDD history and age are static. Mostly non-actionable in 72 hours (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Living alone, relationship breakdown, weight loss
options.rationale: Living alone overlaps with isolation but the others listed (recent breakup, weight loss) are largely not modifiable in the immediate window. Less precise than option A (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: PHQ-9 score, family history, antidepressant non-response
options.rationale: PHQ-9 score and family history are descriptors not levers; antidepressant strategy can be addressed but is not the highest-yield short-window action. Would miss means restriction (Source: RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: Suicide risk factors split into static (unchangeable historical or demographic features) and dynamic (modifiable current circumstances). Effective short-term suicide prevention targets dynamic factors: means restriction, substance use reduction, social connection, and treatment of the underlying mood disorder. The Mann et al 2005 systematic review identified means restriction as one of two interventions with population-level evidence for reducing suicide.
key_points: Static factors set baseline; dynamic factors set the intervention plan
Means restriction has strongest population-level evidence
Substance use is a potent dynamic factor in suicide attempts
Social isolation is modifiable through structured contact
Reassess dynamic risk at each visit
explanation.pearls: Tricyclics are markedly more lethal in overdose than SSRIs; reviewing the home medication cupboard is a discrete but high-yield intervention.

subtopic_detail: Dynamic vs static factor discrimination for short-window intervention

stem: A 56-year-old male farmer presents to a regional ED in NSW. He has had 4 months of progressively worsening depression after losing livestock to drought. He admits passive suicidal ideation, denies a current plan, but acknowledges he has rifles at home (legally licensed under Category A and B firearms licences) for property management. His wife is present and supportive. He has no past psychiatric history, no substance misuse, and is amenable to outpatient treatment with weekly GP follow-up plus referral to the rural mental health team. The ED registrar is preparing to discharge him with a safety plan.

data_table.Examination: Mood low, reactive affect, no psychotic features, no current intent or plan.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 21; collateral from wife confirms account.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate means-restriction intervention before discharge?

options.text: Arrange for firearms to be removed from the property and stored with a licensed third party, documented in the discharge plan
options.rationale: Means restriction is one of the few interventions with population-level evidence to reduce suicide. Australian firearms law (NSW Firearms Act 1996) permits voluntary surrender or third-party storage; engaging the patient and family in voluntary removal and documenting it is the appropriate step, preserving the therapeutic alliance while addressing the most lethal accessible method.

options.text: Advise his wife to hide the rifles within the family home
options.rationale: Hiding within the home is poorly effective because patients commonly know all storage locations; complete removal off-site is required. Would be inadequate (Source: Mann et al 2005).

options.text: Apply for police to confiscate his firearms licence under involuntary provisions
options.rationale: Police-led confiscation is reserved for situations where voluntary removal is refused or imminent risk exists; voluntary is preferred and preserves alliance. Would be a second-line escalation (Source: NSW Firearms Act).

options.text: Document that firearms have been discussed and continue with planned discharge
options.rationale: Documentation without action does not restrict means; access to a firearm in suicidal ideation is a critical actionable factor. Would be inadequate (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Defer means-restriction discussion to the outpatient GP follow-up
options.rationale: Means restriction is most effective in the highest-risk window, which includes the immediate post-discharge period; deferral abandons the window of opportunity. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health Framework).

explanation.summary: Means restriction reduces suicide deaths at both individual and population level (Mann et al 2005 JAMA). In rural Australian populations with firearms access, voluntary off-site storage by a licensed third party (firearms dealer, family member with a safe and licence, or police facility) is preferred. Hiding in-home is ineffective; involuntary licence cancellation is reserved for refusal or imminent danger. Voluntary surrender preserves the therapeutic alliance and is permitted under the NSW Firearms Act 1996.
key_points: Means restriction has population-level evidence for suicide reduction
Voluntary off-site removal preferred over in-home hiding
Rural Australian male farmer cohort has the highest national suicide rate
Engage family and document the plan
Post-discharge window is highest-risk; act now, not at follow-up
explanation.pearls: Suicide method substitution is incomplete: when firearms are restricted, total suicide deaths fall rather than fully shifting to other methods.

sources.label: Mann JJ et al. Suicide prevention strategies. JAMA 2005

subtopic_detail: Firearm means restriction in rural Australian presentation

stem: A 34-year-old woman is being discharged from a Hunter New England LHD ED after assessment for suicidal ideation in the context of a recent relationship breakdown. She has agreed to outpatient follow-up and her parents have driven to the ED to take her home for the night. The ED social worker plans to complete a Safety Planning Intervention before discharge. The patient asks what this will involve.

data_table.Examination: Settled, engaged, agreed to follow-up.
data_table.Investigations: Mental state examination unremarkable for active psychosis.

lead_in: Which sequence most accurately describes the Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention steps?

options.text: Warning signs, internal coping, social distractions, people for support, professional contacts, means restriction
options.rationale: The Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention is a six-step, brief, collaborative intervention with this exact sequence: (1) warning signs, (2) internal coping strategies, (3) social contacts and settings for distraction, (4) people to ask for help, (5) professional and crisis contacts, (6) means restriction. Stanley & Brown 2012 published the original protocol; a 2018 cohort study (Stanley et al) showed reduced suicidal behaviour at 6 months in ED-discharged adults.

options.text: Diagnostic formulation, motivational interviewing, follow-up booking, family meeting
options.rationale: These are components of comprehensive care but not the Stanley-Brown SPI structure. Would conflate broader discharge planning with the SPI tool (Source: Stanley & Brown 2012).

options.text: No-suicide contract, hotline number, follow-up appointment
options.rationale: No-suicide contracts have no evidence of effectiveness and are explicitly discouraged in current guidance; SPI is structurally and conceptually different. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Risk stratification, hospitalisation decision, observation level
options.rationale: These are disposition decisions, not the SPI. Would mistake the assessment-stratification step for the planning intervention (Source: Stanley & Brown 2012).

options.text: CBT formulation, behavioural activation schedule, sleep hygiene checklist
options.rationale: These are therapy interventions delivered over weeks, not the brief safety plan completed at discharge. Would be wrong (Source: NICE Self-Harm guideline).

explanation.summary: The Safety Planning Intervention developed by Stanley and Brown (2012) is a brief, collaborative, six-step plan completed at discharge. It does not require crisis insight but builds a written ladder of internal then external supports culminating in means restriction. RCT and cohort evidence supports a reduction in suicidal behaviour at 6 months compared with usual care. No-suicide contracts are NOT a SPI and have no evidence base.
key_points: Six steps in order: warning signs -> internal coping -> social distraction -> people -> professionals -> means restriction
Brief (30-45 min) and collaborative
Means restriction is the closing step, not optional
No-suicide contracts are deprecated
Stanley et al 2018 JAMA Psychiatry showed reduced suicidal behaviour
explanation.pearls: Carry a written copy AND give a photo to keep on phone; the act of co-creating it is therapeutic.

sources.label: Stanley B, Brown GK. Safety Planning Intervention. Cogn Behav Pract 2012
Stanley B et al. Comparison of SPI vs usual care. JAMA Psychiatry 2018

subtopic_detail: Six-step Stanley-Brown SPI structure

stem: A junior medical officer asks during a teaching session at a Hunter New England LHD mental health unit whether she should document a 'no-suicide contract' as part of her assessments for patients with suicidal ideation. She has seen this used in older clinical notes and wants to know whether to continue the practice.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.

lead_in: Which response most accurately reflects current Australian guidance?

options.text: No-suicide contracts lack evidence, create false reassurance, and have no place in current practice; use a Safety Planning Intervention instead
options.rationale: RANZCP, NICE and NSW Health guidance all advise against no-suicide contracts: they have no evidence of efficacy, create false reassurance for the clinician, lack legal force, and shift responsibility unfairly to the patient. The Safety Planning Intervention has positive evidence and should be used instead.

options.text: Use them only with patients who have insight and capacity
options.rationale: Even in patients with insight, no-suicide contracts have no evidence base and create false reassurance. Would perpetuate a deprecated practice (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Use them as a legal protection mechanism for the treating team
options.rationale: No-suicide contracts carry no legal weight and may worsen documentation quality by substituting for substantive risk assessment. Would be wrong (Source: medico-legal commentary).

options.text: Use them for adolescents but not adults
options.rationale: Evidence and guidance are consistent across age groups: do not use. Would be wrong (Source: NICE NG225).

options.text: Use them when a patient declines to engage in a safety plan
options.rationale: If a patient declines safety planning, that itself is a clinical signal requiring further engagement rather than substitution with an ineffective tool. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: No-suicide contracts (also called 'no-harm contracts') asked patients to agree not to act on suicidal thoughts. They are not endorsed by RANZCP, NICE or NSW Health: no evidence of effectiveness, false reassurance, no legal protection, and they shift responsibility to the patient. The Safety Planning Intervention (Stanley-Brown) is the evidence-supported replacement.
key_points: No-suicide contracts are deprecated across Australian and international guidance
They provide no legal protection
They create false reassurance and may worsen clinical documentation
Replace with Safety Planning Intervention
Document the substantive plan, not a contract
explanation.pearls: If a clinician feels the urge to ask for a contract, that urge is a marker that more comprehensive safety planning is needed.

subtopic_detail: Deprecated no-suicide contracts vs current SPI

stem: A 26-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder presents for the fifth time this year to a Central Coast LHD emergency department after superficial cutting. She has chronic suicidal ideation, multiple past low-lethality overdoses, and is currently engaged in weekly dialectical behaviour therapy. She arrived voluntarily, brought by her DBT therapist who is now in the waiting room. She is calm, denies acute intent to die, and asks to go home. There is no acute decompensation in her presentation compared with baseline, and her DBT therapist confirms continued engagement and an active safety plan.

data_table.Examination: Calm, dressed wounds, mental state at baseline per therapist.

lead_in: Which disposition is most consistent with evidence-based BPD care?

options.text: Discharge to home with continuation of DBT and brief contact with her therapist tonight
options.rationale: Repeated hospitalisation for chronic suicidality in BPD without acute decompensation is associated with worse long-term outcomes, regression and reinforcement of help-seeking via self-harm. NHMRC and RANZCP guidance support brief assessment, validation, contact with the existing therapist, and discharge to the established outpatient framework. Acute change beyond baseline would shift this calculus.

options.text: Admit to the acute inpatient mental health unit for several days
options.rationale: Inpatient admission for chronic non-acute BPD suicidality has limited evidence and may iatrogenically reinforce admission-seeking via self-harm. Would be wrong without acute change (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline).

options.text: Initiate involuntary detention under the NSW Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Personality disorder alone is not grounds for involuntary detention under the NSW MHA 2007; threshold not met. Would be wrong on legal grounds (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

options.text: Commence SSRI in the ED as first-line management of BPD suicidality
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for core BPD features; psychotherapy is. SSRIs have limited evidence in BPD. Would be wrong (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline).

options.text: Discharge with one-off PRN quetiapine 100 mg for self-harm urges
options.rationale: Quetiapine PRN for BPD self-harm has limited evidence and risks longitudinal sedation use, weight gain and dependence concerns. Would be wrong as discharge medication (Source: AMH; NHMRC).

explanation.summary: BPD is associated with chronic suicidal ideation and recurrent self-harm; lifetime risk of suicide is approximately 8-10% but is poorly predicted by individual presentations. Evidence-based care discriminates baseline chronic ideation (manage in established outpatient framework, brief ED interventions, contact existing therapist) from acute decompensation (consider brief admission). Repeated admission for chronic ideation worsens outcomes via regression, iatrogenic identity reinforcement and missed opportunity to use established skills.
key_points: BPD suicide lifetime risk 8-10% but poorly predicted by individual presentations
Distinguish chronic baseline from acute decompensation
DBT is first-line; admission rarely changes long-term trajectory
Personality disorder alone is not grounds for NSW MHA detention
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive, not first-line
explanation.pearls: The therapeutic move in BPD ED presentations is validation + recommit to skills + contact existing supports + brief disposition, not admission.

subtopic_detail: Chronic BPD suicidality vs acute decompensation - disposition

stem: During a tutorial on suicide epidemiology, a Y4 medical student asks how lifetime suicide risk compares across major mental illnesses. The tutor wants the student to understand the relative magnitudes of risk so that disposition planning can be appropriately calibrated.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.

lead_in: Which set of figures most accurately reflects approximate lifetime suicide risk by diagnosis?

options.text: Major depressive disorder ~6%, schizophrenia ~5%, borderline personality disorder ~8%, bipolar disorder ~6%
options.rationale: Inskip et al 1998 meta-analysis (revised by Bostwick & Pankratz 2000) gives lifetime suicide risk approximately 6% for major depression, 5% for schizophrenia, and 6% for bipolar disorder; the NHMRC BPD CPG cites approximately 8-10% for borderline personality disorder. These figures are widely taught and inform disposition decisions, though individual risk is poorly predicted by diagnosis alone.
source_refs: Inskip HM et al. Lifetime risk of suicide. Br J Psychiatry 1998
Bostwick JM & Pankratz VS. Am J Psychiatry 2000

options.text: Major depressive disorder ~15%, schizophrenia ~2%, BPD ~1%, bipolar disorder ~3%
options.rationale: The original 15% figure for MDD (Guze & Robins 1970) was revised down by Bostwick & Pankratz to ~6%; BPD risk is markedly higher than 1%. Would be outdated (Source: Bostwick & Pankratz 2000).

options.text: Major depressive disorder ~30%, schizophrenia ~20%, BPD ~25%, bipolar disorder ~25%
options.rationale: All figures markedly overestimated; total population suicide mortality across all mental illness is far below these numbers. Would be wrong (Source: Inskip et al).

options.text: Major depressive disorder ~1%, schizophrenia ~10%, BPD ~3%, bipolar disorder ~2%
options.rationale: Schizophrenia risk overestimated and MDD substantially underestimated; pattern does not match meta-analytic estimates. Would be wrong (Source: Bostwick & Pankratz 2000).

options.text: All diagnoses carry approximately equal lifetime risk of about 2%
options.rationale: Lifetime risk varies meaningfully by diagnosis and clinical context; uniform 2% is not supported. Would be wrong (Source: Inskip et al).

explanation.summary: Bostwick & Pankratz (Am J Psychiatry 2000) re-analysed lifetime suicide risk by linking inception cohort vs prevalence-based studies and showed earlier figures (15% for MDD) overestimated by mixing inpatient with outpatient cohorts. Recalculated estimates: ~6% MDD, ~5% schizophrenia, ~6% bipolar disorder, with NHMRC giving ~8-10% for BPD. These population estimates inform service planning and clinician calibration but do not predict individual outcomes.
key_points: MDD lifetime suicide risk ~6% (revised from 15%)
Schizophrenia ~5%
Bipolar disorder ~6%
Borderline PD ~8-10%
Diagnosis informs base rate but does not predict individual risk
explanation.pearls: Among psychiatric diagnoses, BPD carries the highest lifetime suicide risk by these estimates, despite individual presentations often appearing 'less severe' than psychotic illness.

sources.label: Bostwick JM, Pankratz VS. Affective disorders and suicide risk. Am J Psychiatry 2000
Inskip HM et al. Br J Psychiatry 1998

subtopic_detail: Lifetime suicide risk by major diagnosis - revised estimates

stem: A 31-year-old woman is being discharged from a Hunter New England LHD acute mental health unit following a 5-day admission for major depression with suicidal ideation. She is now euthymic on mirtazapine 30 mg nocte, has insight, has completed a Safety Planning Intervention, and her partner is present. The ward consultant is finalising the post-discharge plan and wants follow-up timing aligned with current evidence.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, future-oriented, no current ideation.

lead_in: Which post-discharge follow-up plan most closely matches Australian guidance to minimise the early post-discharge suicide-risk window?

options.text: Community mental health team contact within 7 days, ideally within 72 hours, plus a brief contact intervention within 24-48 hours
options.rationale: The post-discharge window (first 7-30 days) is the highest-risk period for suicide after inpatient psychiatric care; RANZCP and NSW Health guidance specify community follow-up within 7 days, ideally within 72 hours. Brief contact interventions (postcards, phone, text) within 24-48 hours have evidence (Carter et al, BMJ 2005, and replications) for reducing repetition of self-harm.
source_refs: NSW Health Mental Health Discharge Planning policy
Carter GL et al. Postcards from the Edge. BMJ 2005

options.text: Routine GP review within 4 weeks; no specialist follow-up unless symptoms recur
options.rationale: Four weeks is outside the highest-risk window and lacks specialist continuity; this plan misses the early post-discharge spike. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Patient to self-refer if symptoms return; no scheduled follow-up
options.rationale: Self-referral as the sole pathway is inadequate; the highest-risk window requires proactive contact. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health Discharge Planning).

options.text: Re-admit weekly for the first month for safety review
options.rationale: Repeated admission is not evidence-based and disrupts community reintegration; outpatient follow-up is preferred. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

options.text: Defer all follow-up until medication review at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Six weeks is far outside the high-risk window; deferral abandons the most consequential period. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: Suicide rates are highest in the first week after psychiatric discharge and remain elevated for at least 30 days. NSW Health and RANZCP specify follow-up within 7 days, preferably 72 hours, with a documented community mental health team contact. Brief contact interventions (Carter postcards, SMS, phone) within 24-48 hours have RCT evidence for reducing repeat self-harm.
key_points: Highest suicide risk window: first 7-30 days post-discharge
Follow-up within 7 days, preferably 72 hours
Brief contact within 24-48 h reduces repeat self-harm (RCT evidence)
Document follow-up booking before discharge
Communicate with GP and partner/family with consent
explanation.pearls: Standardised SMS contact at 1, 2, 4, 8 weeks (digital adaptation of the Carter postcard intervention) is being rolled out across NSW services.

sources.label: Carter GL et al. Postcards from the Edge. BMJ 2005

subtopic_detail: Post-discharge follow-up timing and brief contact interventions

stem: A 19-year-old woman is brought to a Central Coast LHD ED 4 hours after ingesting 30 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg following an argument with her boyfriend. She presented herself when she changed her mind. She is medically stable, an antidote pathway is in train, and her psychiatric assessment is being done in parallel. She tells the psychiatry registrar she did not really want to die, has no plan to repeat, and would like to go home now. She has no past psychiatric history.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented, calm, no abnormality on MSE.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level pending; ALT 25, INR 1.0; NAC running.

lead_in: Which is the most important next consideration in her psychiatric assessment?

options.text: Determine whether what she did at the time of the overdose reflected serious intent regardless of her current minimisation
options.rationale: Post-attempt minimisation is common and may reflect embarrassment, dissociation, partial cognitive recovery, or true ambivalence. The assessment must reconstruct intent at the time of the act (planning, isolation chosen, note left, expectation of being found, expected lethality belief) rather than relying solely on retrospective self-report. The Pierce Suicide Intent Scale operationalises this distinction.
source_refs: NSW Health Suicide Risk Assessment Framework

options.text: Discharge her if she now denies intent and has no past psychiatric history
options.rationale: Discharge based on current denial alone misses the intent assessment at the time of the act, which is the more reliable predictor. Would be unsafe (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Refer to the substance use service as the priority issue
options.rationale: Substance use was not described in the stem; primary need is reconstructing intent. Would miss the immediate priority (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Document a low-risk assessment given the impulsive context
options.rationale: Categorical risk stratification is deprecated, and impulsive attempts do not equate to low risk - impulsive overdoses can be lethal. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Wait until paracetamol level returns to determine psychiatric disposition
options.rationale: Psychiatric and medical assessments run in parallel; psychiatric disposition does not depend on the paracetamol level. Would delay assessment unnecessarily (Source: NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Intent at the time of the act is more reliably assessed by reconstructing circumstances (Pierce 1977 SIS: isolation, timing, prevention of discovery, note, final acts, expectation of lethality) than by retrospective self-report. Minimisation is common post-event. Medical and psychiatric assessments proceed in parallel; the paracetamol level dictates antidote pathway but not psychiatric disposition.
key_points: Reconstruct intent at the time of the act, not retrospectively
Pierce Suicide Intent Scale operationalises this assessment
Minimisation post-event is common and not reassuring on its own
Impulsive attempts can be lethal
Medical and psychiatric assessment run in parallel
explanation.pearls: Lethality belief (what the patient thought 30 tablets would do) is more diagnostic than actual pharmacological lethality.

sources.label: Pierce DW. Suicidal Intent Scale. Br J Psychiatry 1977

subtopic_detail: Reconstructing intent vs accepting post-event minimisation

stem: A 17-year-old presents to her GP at a Central Coast LHD primary care clinic with moderate major depressive disorder. After discussion she agrees to start fluoxetine 10 mg daily, titrating to 20 mg. Her mother asks about the regulatory warning regarding suicidality in young people on antidepressants and wants to know what monitoring will occur.

data_table.Examination: PHQ-9 16; no current suicidal ideation; supportive family.

lead_in: Which monitoring plan best reflects current Australian guidance for adolescent SSRI initiation?

options.text: Review at 1-2 weeks, then weekly contact for the first month, with clear instructions on what symptoms warrant urgent re-contact
options.rationale: The TGA Black Triangle suicidality warning for SSRIs in under-25s reflects pooled data showing a small absolute increase in suicidal thoughts and behaviour, not completed suicide, especially in the first weeks. Australian practice (RANZCP, eTG, TGA) requires early and frequent review (within 1-2 weeks, then at least weekly through the first month), with explicit warning-sign education for patient and family.

options.text: Routine review at 6 weeks to assess therapeutic response
options.rationale: Six-week first review is insufficient for adolescent SSRI initiation; the highest-risk window for behavioural activation and increased ideation is the first 2-4 weeks. Would be wrong (Source: TGA).

options.text: Avoid antidepressants in adolescents entirely
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has the best evidence in adolescent depression (TADS study) and is recommended alongside therapy; avoidance would deny effective treatment. Would be wrong (Source: TADS; eTG).

options.text: Use a tricyclic antidepressant instead given the youth warning on SSRIs
options.rationale: Tricyclics have worse efficacy and lethality-in-overdose profile in adolescents; SSRIs (especially fluoxetine) are first-line. Would be wrong (Source: AMH; eTG).

options.text: Defer review until the patient or family contact spontaneously
options.rationale: Proactive scheduled contact is required, not reactive self-referral; this is the entire point of the monitoring framework. Would be wrong (Source: TGA).

explanation.summary: Pooled adolescent and young adult SSRI trial data show a small absolute increase in suicidal thoughts and non-fatal self-harm, not in completed suicide. TGA Black Triangle and FDA boxed warning prompt close early review. Fluoxetine remains first-line in under-25s due to TADS study evidence. Combined with psychological therapy outperforms either alone.
key_points: Small absolute increase in ideation/self-harm in under-25s, not completed suicide
Fluoxetine first-line in adolescent MDD (TADS)
Review at 1-2 weeks, then weekly for month one
Educate patient AND family on warning signs
Combine with psychological therapy
explanation.pearls: Behavioural activation occurs before mood lift on SSRI - patients may have energy to act on pre-existing ideation; this is the mechanism behind the early-treatment risk.

sources.label: TADS Team. Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study. JAMA 2004

subtopic_detail: Adolescent SSRI monitoring under TGA suicidality advisory

stem: A 38-year-old man presents to a Hunter New England LHD ED with suicidal ideation. He insists his family must not be told he is here, refuses consent for collateral history from his wife who has phoned the ED asking about him, and is otherwise engaging with assessment. He has a known history of recurrent MDD, no current substance use, and a previous near-fatal overdose 3 years ago. The ED registrar asks how to handle the family's call.

data_table.Examination: Withdrawn but cooperative; expressing passive death wish, no plan disclosed.

lead_in: Which response best balances confidentiality with safety obligations?

options.text: Acknowledge the patient's confidentiality preference, explore the reasons for refusal, and explain that information may be received from the family without breaching confidentiality, even if disclosure to them is restricted
options.rationale: Receiving collateral information from family is permitted without breaching confidentiality; the clinician is obtaining data, not disclosing. Exploring the reasons for refusal (shame, family conflict, fear of impact on others) often resolves the impasse. Disclosure of information to family is restricted by consent unless an over-riding risk threshold is met under the NSW Privacy and Personal Information Protection Act and the Health Records and Information Privacy Act.

options.text: Refuse to take any information from the wife to honour confidentiality
options.rationale: Receiving information is not a breach; this confuses receiving with disclosing. Would refuse important collateral data unnecessarily (Source: RANZCP Ethics).

options.text: Disclose his presence and full details to the wife immediately to enable family support
options.rationale: Disclosure without consent breaches privacy unless the risk threshold is met; even then, the disclosure is the minimum required. Would be wrong (Source: NSW HRIP Act).

options.text: Confirm his presence but withhold clinical details
options.rationale: Confirming presence is still a disclosure without consent; this incrementally breaches confidentiality. Would be wrong unless risk threshold met (Source: NSW Privacy law).

options.text: Defer the call to the on-call psychiatry registrar to decide
options.rationale: The ED clinician is responsible for the immediate handling; deferring without action does not address the call or the patient's care. Would be wrong as the sole approach (Source: NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Confidentiality applies to disclosure, not receipt. Receiving collateral information from family is appropriate and does not breach confidentiality; disclosure of the patient's presence or clinical information requires consent or an over-riding risk-based override. NSW health information privacy law (HRIP Act) permits disclosure where reasonably necessary to lessen or prevent a serious and imminent threat to life or health, but the disclosure must be the minimum required.
key_points: Receiving information is not the same as disclosing
Explore reasons for refusal - often resolves the impasse
Disclosure requires consent or a risk-based override
NSW HRIP Act permits limited disclosure for serious imminent threat
Document the reasoning
explanation.pearls: Document explicitly what was received from whom, and what was (and was not) disclosed back; this is a common source of complaints.

sources.label: NSW Health Records and Information Privacy Act 2002

subtopic_detail: Collateral history vs confidentiality - receiving vs disclosing

stem: A 24-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia presents to a Hunter New England LHD ED accompanied by his case manager. He reports hearing the voice of a deceased neighbour telling him to walk in front of a train. He denies wanting to die but feels the voice is becoming more difficult to resist. He is currently on risperidone 4 mg daily, started 5 weeks ago, with partial improvement in positive symptoms. He has no history of substance use, lives with his parents, and his case manager confirms compliance.

data_table.Examination: Thought disorder mild, persecutory delusion present, command auditory hallucination as described, no insight that the voice is illness-driven.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate disposition?

options.text: Admit to an inpatient mental health unit for medication review and close observation
options.rationale: Command hallucinations to harm self in the context of partial-response first-episode psychosis carry materially elevated risk, particularly with limited insight that the voice is illness-generated. Inpatient admission is justified for safety, medication optimisation (risperidone dose review or switch, consider clozapine pathway if criteria emerge), and close observation. The combination of first-episode + male sex + command hallucinations is associated with higher suicide risk early in illness.
source_refs: Hor & Taylor 2010 suicide in schizophrenia review

options.text: Discharge with twice-weekly case manager contact and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Active command hallucinations to harm self with poor insight exceed the threshold for safe community management at this point. Would be unsafe (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Add lorazepam 1 mg PRN and discharge home
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine PRN does not address the command hallucination or its driver; antipsychotic optimisation and observation are needed. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Wait for the on-call psychiatry consultant to ring back tomorrow
options.rationale: Waiting overnight with active command hallucinations to harm self is unsafe; immediate disposition is required. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

options.text: Switch to oral quetiapine in the ED before disposition
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotic in the ED without specialist input and observation does not change immediate risk; safe environment first. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

explanation.summary: Suicide risk in schizophrenia is concentrated early in illness and is elevated by command hallucinations to harm self, male sex, depressive features, recent hospital discharge, and poor insight. Hor & Taylor's 2010 review identifies these factors. First-episode patients with command hallucinations and limited insight warrant inpatient assessment for safety and medication optimisation. The risk does not respond reliably to benzodiazepine sedation; it responds to better psychosis control plus environmental safety.
key_points: First-episode is highest-risk window for suicide in schizophrenia
Command hallucinations + poor insight = inpatient threshold
Optimise antipsychotic; consider clozapine pathway later if criteria met
Suicide in schizophrenia is often driven by depressive symptoms
Benzodiazepines do not treat the underlying driver
explanation.pearls: Clozapine is the only antipsychotic with FDA approval to reduce suicidality in schizophrenia (InterSePT trial); consider downstream if treatment-resistance criteria evolve.

sources.label: Hor K, Taylor M. Suicide and schizophrenia. J Psychopharmacol 2010
Meltzer HY et al. Clozapine for suicidality (InterSePT). Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003

subtopic_detail: Command hallucinations in first-episode schizophrenia - disposition

stem: A medical student on a geriatric medicine placement at a Hunter New England LHD hospital asks about suicide risk in older adults. The consultant wants the student to understand both the demographics and the modifiable contributors.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects Australian epidemiology of late-life suicide?

options.text: Men aged 85+ have the highest age-specific suicide rate in Australia; presentations tend to involve higher intent and higher lethality methods
options.rationale: AIHW data consistently show the highest age-specific suicide rate in Australia is in men aged 85 and over. Late-life suicide presentations differ from younger adults: fewer warning attempts, higher intent at first attempt, more lethal methods, and more frequent association with physical illness, social isolation, recent loss, and depression that is often under-recognised in primary care.
source_refs: Conwell Y et al. Suicide in older adults. Psychiatr Clin North Am 2011

options.text: Late-life suicide rates are lower than in adolescents and require less screening
options.rationale: Age-specific rates are highest in older men; screening should be heightened, not relaxed. Would be wrong (Source: AIHW).

options.text: Most late-life suicides occur in patients with no contact with a healthcare system in the year prior
options.rationale: Most older adults who die by suicide have had primary care contact in the weeks before death, representing a key intervention point. Would be wrong (Source: Conwell).

options.text: Antidepressants are contraindicated in older adults with suicidal ideation due to bleeding risk
options.rationale: Antidepressants (preferably SSRI like sertraline or escitalopram, with PPI cover where bleeding risk is present) are recommended for late-life depression. Would be wrong (Source: AMH; eTG).

options.text: Older adults are at highest risk in long-term institutional care rather than at home
options.rationale: Most late-life suicides occur in the community, often at home, not in institutional settings. Would be wrong (Source: AIHW).

explanation.summary: Older Australian men have the highest age-specific suicide rate. Late-life suicide presentations differ: less warning, higher intent, more lethal methods, and frequent under-recognition of depression in primary care. Most older adults who die by suicide had primary care contact in the preceding weeks. Intervention targets: depression treatment, pain control, social connection, means restriction, and family education.
key_points: Highest age-specific rate: men aged 85+
Higher intent and lethality per attempt than younger adults
Primary care contact common in preceding weeks - intervention point
Pain, isolation and loss are key dynamic factors
Depression often under-recognised - consider GDS-15 screening
explanation.pearls: Sertraline and escitalopram have the most favourable older-adult profiles (lowest interaction burden, manageable hyponatraemia risk).

sources.label: Conwell Y. Suicide in older adults. Psychiatr Clin North Am 2011

subtopic_detail: Late-life suicide epidemiology and clinical features

stem: A 47-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is reviewed in a Central Coast LHD outpatient psychiatry clinic. She is currently euthymic on quetiapine 400 mg nocte. She has had two prior episodes of suicidal ideation during depressive phases and the consultant is considering switching to lithium for maintenance, partly for mood stability and partly for anti-suicide effect. She asks how strong the anti-suicide evidence is.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, future-oriented.
data_table.Investigations: TFT, U&E, eGFR within normal limits; no contraindications to lithium identified.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the evidence for lithium's anti-suicide effect in bipolar disorder?

options.text: Lithium reduces suicide and self-harm beyond its mood-stabilising effect; this is independently supported in meta-analyses and is a recognised indication for use
options.rationale: Cipriani et al 2013 BMJ meta-analysis demonstrated lithium reduces deaths from suicide and total deaths in mood disorders, with an effect over and above mood stabilisation. RANZCP and Cochrane reviews concur. The effect is most clearly demonstrated in bipolar disorder but is also seen in unipolar depression. Australian guidance positions this as an active reason to choose lithium when suicide risk is a feature.
source_refs: Cipriani A et al. BMJ 2013 lithium and suicide meta-analysis

options.text: Lithium reduces suicide only because it stabilises mood; no independent effect has been shown
options.rationale: Meta-analyses suggest an effect over and above mood stabilisation, possibly via serotonergic and aggression-reducing mechanisms. Would be wrong (Source: Cipriani 2013).

options.text: Lithium has no proven effect on suicide and is only indicated for acute mania
options.rationale: Lithium is established for maintenance, has anti-suicide evidence, and is not limited to acute mania. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Lithium increases suicide risk due to its narrow therapeutic index
options.rationale: Although lithium has a narrow therapeutic index requiring monitoring, the net effect on suicide is protective in the cohorts studied. Would be wrong (Source: Cipriani 2013).

options.text: Lithium's anti-suicide effect is restricted to women with rapid-cycling presentations
options.rationale: The effect is broader than this single subgroup, across bipolar (and to some extent unipolar) populations. Would be wrong (Source: Cipriani 2013).

explanation.summary: Lithium is the most consistently supported pharmacological agent with anti-suicide effect in mood disorders. Cipriani's 2013 BMJ meta-analysis (48 trials, 6674 participants) demonstrated reduction in deaths from suicide compared with placebo. The mechanism is partly via mood stabilisation but appears to extend beyond it, possibly involving serotonin and impulsivity pathways. RANZCP and AMH recognise this in maintenance treatment choice.
key_points: Cipriani 2013 BMJ meta-analysis: reduced suicide deaths on lithium
Effect over and above mood stabilisation
Recognised indication in bipolar maintenance
Requires monitoring (lithium level, TFT, eGFR)
Net protective despite narrow therapeutic index
explanation.pearls: Counsel about lithium toxicity precipitants (dehydration, NSAIDs, ACEIs, diuretics, vomiting/diarrhoea) at every dispensing point; toxicity itself is a clinical risk that has driven prescribing decline despite the evidence.

sources.label: Cipriani A et al. Lithium and suicide. BMJ 2013

subtopic_detail: Lithium anti-suicide evidence in bipolar maintenance

stem: A 28-year-old woman attends her GP at a Hunter New England LHD community clinic for her 6-week postnatal review. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 17. On Question 10 ('The thought of harming myself has occurred to me') she has answered 'Sometimes'. She has a 6-week-old breastfed infant, is sleeping in 2-hour blocks, denies a plan to act, and her husband is supportive but works long hours.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, mood low, no psychomotor retardation, no psychotic features.
data_table.Investigations: TFT pending; ferritin pending; B12 pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Direct follow-up with structured suicide risk assessment, safety planning including infant care arrangements, and same-week perinatal mental health referral
options.rationale: A positive EPDS Q10 ('sometimes' or higher) requires direct same-visit follow-up with structured assessment, safety planning that explicitly addresses infant care contingencies, and same-week perinatal mental health referral per NSW Health Perinatal Mental Health Framework and COPE guidance. Sleep deprivation, primiparous status, and limited daytime support are dynamic factors to address.
source_refs: NSW Health SAFE START Perinatal Mental Health
COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2017

options.text: Repeat EPDS in 2 weeks and refer if Q10 remains positive
options.rationale: A positive Q10 requires same-visit follow-up; delay loses the intervention window and is unsafe. Would be wrong (Source: COPE 2017).

options.text: Start an SSRI and re-screen in 4 weeks
options.rationale: SSRI may be appropriate but does not substitute for structured risk assessment and safety planning today. Would be incomplete (Source: COPE 2017).

options.text: Reassure that postpartum baby blues commonly include such thoughts
options.rationale: EPDS 17 with positive Q10 at 6 weeks is not baby blues; reassurance trivialises and misses the threshold for action. Would be wrong (Source: COPE).

options.text: Recommend ceasing breastfeeding to improve sleep and reassess
options.rationale: Ceasing breastfeeding is not the right primary intervention and may worsen distress; sleep can be supported in other ways. Would be wrong (Source: COPE; ABM).

explanation.summary: EPDS Question 10 is the suicidality item; any positive response (sometimes/hardly ever/yes quite often) requires same-visit structured suicide-risk assessment and safety planning that explicitly addresses infant care contingencies. NSW Health SAFE START and COPE 2017 guidelines specify same-week perinatal mental health referral. Sleep deprivation, primiparity, limited support, and history of mood disorder are dynamic factors.
key_points: Any positive EPDS Q10 = same-visit action
Safety planning must include infant care arrangements
Same-week perinatal mental health referral
Sleep, support, primiparity are dynamic factors
Risk to infant must be screened for separately
explanation.pearls: EPDS misses about 10-20% of postpartum depression; clinical judgement still applies when EPDS is negative but symptoms are present.

sources.label: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2017
NSW Health SAFE START Perinatal Mental Health

subtopic_detail: Positive EPDS Q10 - same-visit response

stem: A 32-year-old woman is brought to a Hunter New England LHD ED by her husband 10 days postpartum. Over the last 48 hours she has barely slept, has paced the house, and tonight told him she 'must save the baby from the demons' by 'sending her back'. She has a family history of bipolar I disorder in her sister. There is a 10-day-old infant at home being cared for by her mother.

data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, fluctuating affect, delusional belief as above, possible auditory hallucinations.
data_table.Investigations: TFT, calcium, glucose normal; B12 normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate plan?

options.text: Admit to a perinatal or mental health unit immediately, ensure infant is not in her unsupervised care, and arrange urgent psychiatric review
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with materially elevated risk of suicide AND infanticide (~4% suicide, ~1-4% infanticide in untreated illness). Immediate admission is required, infant safety must be ensured, and a separate pathway for the infant must be documented (typically with another caregiver under family or community support). Family history of bipolar I is a major risk factor.
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Perinatal Mood and Anxiety Disorders
Sit D et al. Postpartum psychosis review J Womens Health 2006

options.text: Discharge home with husband to monitor and follow up in clinic tomorrow
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is an emergency; community management is unsafe. Would be wrong (Source: COPE).

options.text: Commence sertraline in the ED and discharge
options.rationale: Sertraline is not the right agent (this is psychosis, possibly bipolar-spectrum), and discharge is unsafe. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Arrange voluntary outpatient psychiatry assessment within 7 days
options.rationale: Delay of 7 days is unsafe in postpartum psychosis; same-day inpatient admission is required. Would be wrong (Source: COPE).

options.text: Reassure that postpartum hormone shifts often cause transient psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is not a transient hormonal phenomenon to be reassured about; it is a defined emergency. Would be wrong (Source: COPE; Sit 2006).

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis typically appears in the first 2 weeks postpartum (median day 8), often in the bipolar spectrum, with risks of suicide (~4%) and infanticide (~1-4%) in untreated illness. Immediate admission, infant safety planning, and urgent psychiatric review are mandatory. Personal or family history of bipolar disorder is the most important risk factor (risk approximately 25-50% in women with bipolar I).
key_points: Emergency: admit same-day
Suicide risk ~4%, infanticide risk ~1-4% untreated
Family history of bipolar I is a major risk
Plan infant care under another caregiver
Initial pharmacotherapy typically antipsychotic + mood stabiliser, ECT in severe cases
explanation.pearls: Mother-baby units (rare in Australia) are the gold-standard environment; in their absence, separate planning is required for both mother and infant safety.

sources.label: Sit D et al. Postpartum psychosis. J Womens Health 2006

subtopic_detail: Postpartum psychosis - emergency disposition with dual risk

stem: A 22-year-old Aboriginal man presents to a regional NSW ED with self-harm in the context of recent loss of his uncle. He is not currently engaging in detail with the registrar. The ED has an Aboriginal Health Worker on duty. The registrar is structuring his approach.

data_table.Examination: Withdrawn; superficial laceration to forearm, no acute medical compromise.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first step in his assessment?

options.text: Invite the Aboriginal Health Worker to be present during assessment if the patient agrees, and frame the conversation with attention to grief, family and community context
options.rationale: Culturally safe care in NSW Health is the standard for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander patients. The Aboriginal Health Worker provides cultural support and improves disclosure and engagement; grief over loss of family is a recognised driver of distress in Aboriginal communities and should be acknowledged explicitly. Consent is preserved; presence is offered, not imposed.
source_refs: NSW Health Aboriginal Mental Health Workforce Program
AHRC Wiyi Yani U Thangani report

options.text: Proceed with structured assessment without involving any additional staff to maintain confidentiality
options.rationale: Offering an Aboriginal Health Worker preserves confidentiality (with consent) and improves cultural safety; not involving them is the less safe default. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

options.text: Discharge with referral to GP and avoid distressing him further
options.rationale: Discharge after self-harm without full assessment misses the intervention window; cultural sensitivity does not mean omission of care. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Detain him under the NSW Mental Health Act given the higher suicide rate in Aboriginal communities
options.rationale: Demographic risk does not meet the threshold for detention; the Act requires individual criteria to be met. Would be wrong on legal grounds (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

options.text: Refer to a substance use service as the priority given the positive BAC
options.rationale: BAC 0.02 is not clinically intoxicated; primary need is psychiatric assessment with cultural support. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health).

explanation.summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander young men have a markedly elevated age-specific suicide rate. Culturally safe assessment, ideally with an Aboriginal Health Worker present (with consent), improves engagement and disclosure. Recognition of grief, family and community context, intergenerational trauma, and the social determinants is core. Demographic risk does not meet legal thresholds for involuntary detention.
key_points: Offer Aboriginal Health Worker presence with consent
Recognise grief and family loss as core drivers
Cultural safety is the standard, not the exception
Demographic risk does not meet detention thresholds
Coordinate with Aboriginal community-controlled services
explanation.pearls: 'Sorry business' is the culturally appropriate framing of grief and family obligations after a death; recognising the term in the conversation builds trust.

sources.label: NSW Health Aboriginal Mental Health Workforce Program

subtopic_detail: Culturally safe assessment of Aboriginal young man post self-harm

stem: A 44-year-old woman is admitted to a Hunter New England LHD inpatient mental health unit with treatment-resistant major depressive disorder and severe persistent suicidal ideation despite trials of sertraline, venlafaxine, mirtazapine augmented with quetiapine, and current lithium augmentation at therapeutic level. She has not engaged with ECT due to memory concerns. The consultant raises the possibility of intranasal esketamine (Spravato) under the TGA-approved indication.

data_table.Examination: Severely depressed, persistent active suicidal ideation, slow speech, intact reality testing.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.8 mmol/L; TFT, U&E normal; BP 124/76.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the evidence base and Australian regulatory status of esketamine for suicidal ideation in MDD?

options.text: Intranasal esketamine has TGA approval for treatment-resistant depression and shows rapid (within 24 hours) reduction in suicidal ideation in MDD with active SI, used adjunctively to an oral antidepressant in a structured monitored setting
options.rationale: Esketamine (S-enantiomer of ketamine) was TGA-registered for treatment-resistant depression in 2021. ASPIRE I and II trials (Fu et al, Ionescu et al, J Clin Psychiatry 2020) demonstrated rapid reduction in depressive symptoms in MDD patients with active suicidal ideation within 24 hours, used adjunctively to an oral antidepressant. Administration requires a structured monitored setting (observation for 2 hours post-dose for sedation, dissociation and BP).
source_refs: Fu DJ et al. ASPIRE I. J Clin Psychiatry 2020

options.text: Esketamine is not approved in Australia and remains experimental
options.rationale: Esketamine has been TGA-registered since 2021 for TRD. Would be wrong on regulatory status (Source: TGA).

options.text: Esketamine has a sustained anti-suicidal effect lasting at least 12 weeks after a single dose
options.rationale: The effect peaks rapidly but is not sustained for 12 weeks after a single dose; protocols use repeated dosing with oral antidepressant. Would be wrong (Source: ASPIRE trials).

options.text: Esketamine is administered at home with no in-clinic monitoring required
options.rationale: Administration requires a structured monitored setting (REMS/equivalent) due to sedation, dissociation and transient BP rise. Would be wrong (Source: TGA PI).

options.text: Esketamine is first-line for MDD with suicidality before any antidepressant trial
options.rationale: Esketamine is positioned for treatment-resistant depression, used adjunctively, not as first-line. Would be wrong on positioning (Source: TGA PI).

explanation.summary: Intranasal esketamine (Spravato) is TGA-registered in Australia for treatment-resistant depression (failure of two adequate trials of antidepressants), used adjunctively to an oral antidepressant. The ASPIRE I and II trials demonstrated rapid reduction in depressive symptoms in MDD with active suicidal ideation within 24 hours. Administration requires a structured monitored setting due to dissociation, sedation and transient BP rise. Protocols use induction and maintenance dosing; effect is not sustained from a single dose.
key_points: TGA-registered for TRD (failed >=2 adequate antidepressant trials)
Rapid effect within 24 hours on suicidal ideation
Adjunctive to oral antidepressant, not monotherapy
Monitored setting: 2-hour post-dose observation, BP, sedation, dissociation
Repeated dosing required; no sustained single-dose effect
explanation.pearls: Racemic IV ketamine is also used in some Australian centres for similar indications under specialist protocols but is off-label; intranasal esketamine is the on-label product.

sources.label: Fu DJ et al. ASPIRE I. J Clin Psychiatry 2020

subtopic_detail: Esketamine for suicidal ideation in TRD - TGA status and ASPIRE evidence

stem: A first-year intern is hesitant to ask patients about suicidal ideation, worried that asking 'might give them the idea'. She asks for guidance during an ED shift at a Hunter New England LHD ED.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate response?

options.text: Asking explicitly about suicidal thoughts does not increase suicide risk and improves disclosure
options.rationale: Multiple studies (Dazzi et al 2014 meta-analysis) show no evidence that asking about suicide increases ideation or behaviour; if anything, explicit asking reduces distress and improves disclosure. This is a foundational tenet of suicide risk assessment training in Australia.
source_refs: Dazzi T et al. Psychol Med 2014 - does asking increase risk

options.text: Asking should only be done by senior clinicians
options.rationale: All clinicians who interact with patients should be able to ask; gatekeeping the question to seniors delays disclosure. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Use only indirect language such as 'feeling tired of things'
options.rationale: Indirect language reduces clarity; explicit language is recommended. Would be wrong (Source: Black Dog Institute).

options.text: Avoid asking unless patient raises it first
options.rationale: Many patients do not raise suicide spontaneously due to shame or fear; routine asking is required in high-risk presentations. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Asking is only required if a screening tool flags suicidality
options.rationale: Clinical context drives asking; reliance on screening tools alone misses presentations. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: The 'asking causes suicide' myth has no empirical support and is harmful. Dazzi et al's 2014 meta-analysis (13 studies) found no evidence of increased ideation or behaviour after explicit asking; many studies show reduction in distress and improved disclosure. Explicit language ('Have you had any thoughts of suicide?') is preferred over euphemisms.
key_points: No evidence that asking increases risk; evidence supports the opposite
Explicit language preferred over euphemisms
All clinicians should be able to ask
Routine asking in high-risk presentations, not patient-led only
Practice with simulated patients reduces clinician anxiety
explanation.pearls: Pair the explicit question with normalisation: 'When people are going through this, they sometimes have thoughts of suicide; has that happened for you?'

sources.label: Dazzi T et al. Does asking about suicide increase risk? Psychol Med 2014

subtopic_detail: The 'asking causes suicide' myth and its rebuttal

stem: A medical student journalism club is preparing a campus newsletter article on a recent student death by suicide. They ask for guidance on safe-reporting principles. A Y4 student offers to draft talking points based on national guidance.

data_table.Examination: N/A media-reporting scenario.

lead_in: Which combination most closely reflects Mindframe (Australian safe-reporting) guidance?

options.text: Avoid detailed method description and location, frame the article around help-seeking and support resources, include local crisis line numbers, avoid prominent placement and sensational language
options.rationale: Mindframe (Everymind Australia) safe-reporting guidance addresses the Werther effect (imitative suicide following media coverage) and Papageno effect (protective effect of coverage emphasising coping and help-seeking). Recommendations: avoid method/location detail, avoid sensational language and prominent placement, include help-seeking framing, list local crisis numbers (Lifeline 13 11 14, Suicide Call Back 1300 659 467, Kids Helpline 1800 55 1800).
source_refs: Mindframe Reporting Suicide and Mental Illness
Niederkrotenthaler T et al. Papageno effect. Br J Psychiatry 2010

options.text: Provide detailed method information to raise awareness of warning signs
options.rationale: Detailed method coverage is associated with imitative suicide (Werther effect) and is explicitly discouraged. Would be wrong (Source: Mindframe).

options.text: Use dramatic language to convey the seriousness of the issue
options.rationale: Sensational language is associated with harm; measured factual language is recommended. Would be wrong (Source: Mindframe).

options.text: Publish identifying personal information about the student to humanise the story
options.rationale: Identifying details without family consent breaches privacy and may contribute to imitative harm; family-led storytelling with consent is the alternative. Would be wrong (Source: Mindframe).

options.text: Omit any mention of help-seeking resources to keep the article concise
options.rationale: Help-seeking and support resources are a core protective inclusion (Papageno effect); omission removes the protective dimension. Would be wrong (Source: Mindframe).

explanation.summary: Mindframe (Everymind Australia) is the national guidance for safe reporting and portrayal of suicide. The Werther effect describes increased imitative suicide after detailed media coverage; the Papageno effect describes the protective effect of coverage that emphasises coping, recovery and help-seeking. Practical safe-reporting: no method/location detail, no sensational language, no prominent placement, include help-seeking and crisis line numbers, treat family privacy with consent.
key_points: Werther effect: detailed coverage drives imitative suicide
Papageno effect: coping-and-help-seeking coverage is protective
Avoid method, location, sensational language
Include Lifeline 13 11 14, Suicide Call Back 1300 659 467, Kids Helpline 1800 55 1800
Family consent for any identifying details
explanation.pearls: Even non-media communications (school assemblies, university announcements) follow the same principles; this is not journalism-only guidance.

sources.label: Mindframe Reporting Suicide and Mental Illness
Niederkrotenthaler T et al. Papageno effect. Br J Psychiatry 2010

subtopic_detail: Mindframe safe-reporting principles - Werther vs Papageno
